commentBody: Well done, Rebecca!

Good puzzle today, fun solve.  A lot of clever 'AHA' clues considering it's a Tuesday.

I got all the theme clues before I got 'THIS WON'T END WELL'.  And didn't understand the theme until I read Wordplay.  Maybe a miss on the theme.  But good puzzle.

Very meaty Tuesday puzzle today, and very clever revealer.
I gotta say Rebecca must be like a dog with a bone once she gets a theme she likes. The other puzzle of hers which stands out to me was the Haunted Mansion which was a wonderful theme which required an unusual grid and major witchcraft to make it oft. Today may be just a Tuesday, and not nearly as complicated, but still took a symmetric grid and masterful grid design to make these odd collection of themers fit. So don’t take the design of this puzzle for granted.
Well done.

If you don't want my porterhouse, that'd be a missed steak.

(Maybe just try it? I'll filet bit better.)
A bit too obscure for me

Ms. Goldstein is, by far, one of my favorite constructors, so I'm thankful she's so prolific. A terrific Tuesday puzzle, capped off with Duran Duran's "RIO"... https://youtu.be/nTizYn3-QN0?si=mV028ZZ2b9YQW6oV What would make it even more perfect is if John Ezra were to reappear. Where are you, sir? You're being missed!

Ugh, so very many sports clues.  To borrow from that vernacular - boo.  Not pleasant for me.  (6?  7?)  It helped make this surprisingly challenging for a mere Tuesday, although I can't say I enjoyed it.

🤷‍♂️

commentBody: Tuesday au jus.

Puzzle first, update notes second


I loved this puzzle.  Good mental workout for a Tuesday.  Was AT SEA when I confused Hispaniola for the Iberian peninsula.  Oops. 

As to the update. It’s gonna take some getting used to not having the blog open my safari to look at comments and the blog.  I like to be able to follow some comments so being able to keep open that tab in Safari is helpful.  Is there a way to modify this setting so I can go back to my preference?

Don’t know what prompted me to notice — and perhaps it is quite common in crossword puzzles, due to the frequency of answers that are one-syllable words — but I was struck by the number of them in this puzzle that have homophones:

INDY, AWED, LIAR, WAIST, TONY, SOAR, WOE, SEAR, WAYS, GNU, RECEDE, MEETS, WOK and, in a way, REES and REESE.

(I thought about including EDYS, but Google tells me it is pronounced with a long e.)

Maybe I missed some?  Maybe I should just go to bed.

Anyone willing to explain OHSO … 

The theme was cute. 

Thanks.
I have no beef with this puzzle. It was mutton but fun.

Many commenters seem to love the theme but I have a nit to pick with it.

'How would you like your steak cooked?'

'Well.'

🤷🏽

I was under the impression it needs to be "Well done".

Then there was all the trivia... I didn't need lookups, but the difficulty was Wednesday-ish, for me.

I had an interesting (?) realization, inspired by IRATE. "Irate" and "irritated" sort of look like they share the same root, right? They both refer to intensities of negative emotions, and "ira" is Latin for "anger". Apparently, as per Etymonline, that's not the case. "Irate" does in fact come from "ira", but "irritated" has a different etymology, probably related to the Proto-Italic verb *rito- "stirred," which is the same root that produced English "run". Now, Polish has its own word for "irate", of proto-Slavic origins - it's "wściekły" - but our equivalent of "irritated" - "poirytowany" - has the same etymology as in English.
Up the Chels!!

For 46A, I had HAPPY__DI__. 

HAPPYenDIng? 

I thought, “No. Not the Grey Lady. She’s not going for this”. 

But it does fit the clue.
I don't understand the half-helix clue. Can someone please clarify? I'm no expert on genetics, but as I've understood it, a DNA STRAND is a full double helix, half of which would be an RNA strand.

50D: Lindsey got married at the end of 2024 and now uses her married last name of Heaps. Is it part of the NYT Style Guide to use maiden names for activities that happened before they were married? Or is this a tiny nit in a puzzle I enjoyed?
Tough old Tuesday for this Aussie. Definitely felt like a Wednesday, but we got there in the end
This is the first NYT crossword I have been able to do without needing to check my work! Progress.

commentBody:
I will write this again and try to emu-proof it.

I know it's (reverse) sexist but I don't care.

When I see a woman setter's name atop a solve, I am happier than when I see a man's.

I just think women set more elegant, subtle, enlightening and enlivening grids than do men, all Venn-diagram considerations applied.

At least in these pages.

And over the last 18 months I've been live solving and 10 years of archive spelunking.

So there.

I said it.

What sport is MMA?  This is the second time this 
 has appeared in recent days. I think the puzzle was a nice little challenge for relatively new solvers.

I'll have to add DIMES and USWNT to the ever growing sports term folder in my brain.

I'm familiar with DIMES relating to ratting someone out, as in dropped a dime, presumably using a pay phone to call in an anonymous to the police.  Interesting that it has a positive connotation in basketball.  I guess it makes sense, since the caller is assisting the police.  Unless there's another reason for that term.

Nice toughened up Tuesday, Rebecca.
55A and 48D  made me smile, that is all I can really ask for when solving a crossword puzzle.
Well, be advised that this comment won't end well ... but at least it started that way.
Not related to this puzzle, but can someone tell me if it's possible to look up a constructor on the xwordinfo site? I see a seach box for pre-Shortz constructors, but not for current ones. Thanks.

Reminds me of a good one…
Where do over-explainers get their water? From a Well, actually…

A few laudatory notes re the theme.  

It’s original.  That revealer?  That gorgeous THIS WON’T END WELL?  It’s hard to believe but true that it has never appeared before in any of the major outlets.  High props to Rebecca for fixing that!

Its punchline perfectly caps its set-up.  Not only does WELL superbly follow RAW, RARE, and MEDIUM, but the revealer also superbly puns on “ending well”.  Brava!

It vibrates with spark.  Just look at those theme answers – RUBS RAW, EXCEEDINGLY RARE, HAPPY MEDIUM, and THIS WON’T END WELL.  Bam, bam, bam, bam!

Rebecca not only nailed the theme, but, intentionally or not, surrounded it with echoes:
•	Food echoes: SALAD, WOK, penne ALLA vodka, EDYS.
•	Sparky answer echoes: BLEEP, BOINK, BLOB, SPRAWL, I MEAN C’MON.

This was, for me, not only fun to fill, but a tour of beauty in the box.  A high-quality outing through a high-quality creation.  Thank you, Rebecca!
Well done!
Thought I was on my way to new best time and then got caught by what always seem like simple clues (simple only after I’ve solved) in the very last third of the puzzle. Lovely Tuesday theme.

ABYSS BLEEP SONAR :-) I'm sure the raw-medium-well (done) progression has been used as a theme before, but Claude Levi-Stauss and I liked the inclusion of "raw" into the mix. As it was released in 1954, I was a little young* to have a concert stub from the Jimmy Rowles Trio: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xm-64D_mGzE

This one seemed especially meaty. 

(Let's see how many beat me to this pun.) Solving took me almost 20 minutes, so "meaty" applies doubly for me.

Didn't know REES or REECE, and had REESE initially for the latter. The former I must have filled from the crosses, since I only saw the clue while reviewing after I finished the puzzle.

Liked the two pairs of long vertical entries.

The lateral symmetry was a nice variation on the usual, and made the grid pattern feel especially fresh today. 

Nice Tuesday puzzle!

I finished with one wrong letter--the crossing of the director (unknown person and ?movie?) and the slang usage DIMES.  I had read "Assists" as a noun ("He was credited with three Assists") and I had REED/DIMED.  A longer time spent considering the crossing might have suggested the S, but REED seemed a more likely surname.  
Oh, well.  

As it happens, DHubby grilled two lovely steaks as a "Birthday Dinner" last night.  MEDIUM RARE--which didn't make it to Rebecca Goldstein's list.  

I am not quite sure of MEGA for "Sonic boom generator"..... MEGAton bomb, but as it stands alone in the grid, rather a stretch.

I got both a Server Error at one point, and later a forced sign-in this morning.  Is someone at The NYT upset about the Knicks's loss, or what?
Is it just me, or is this one of the weirdest, most random themes they've used? Not bad, and helpful in solving--just weird.

4/10 Tuesday.

3D was too much for a Tuesday, especially crossing an obscure basketball term that you hear a lot more in football. That particular term has never been used before in the format of an abbreviation and it’s not something people would think of.

MEME is becoming overused. It seems to be in every puzzle as a solution to a different clue.

USWNT I saw complaints, I think it’s fair. Although it felt like a Wednesday or Sunday situation.

BOINK, eh - I mean whatever I guess. 

Otherwise fine. I also recognize that the skill needed to create this crossword is infinitely more than I have and for that I am appreciative and recognize my minor complaints are EXCEEDINGLY (dim witted).

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Dr Francis


Sorry about the delay.  Busy shift.

Everyone gets everything everywhere these days, aside from denizens of straight-up autocracies, no?

I could write a book about your questions.  But I'll write a few bullet points instead.

Just for some context I've worked in EDs for 30 years on three continents, in hospitals from tiny to ginormous, backwaters to metropolises, community to academic. 

I'll skip elaborate dramatic analyses of the shows, but will say I enjoyed the first several seasons of ER as good soap.  Plus it was fun to watch as it started as I finished my residency, so there's that.  Its final seasons were too silly for me medically and turgidly to stick with.

I made it through two episodes of The Pitt before its mountain of saccharine and streams of treacle overwhelmed my ability to maufacture insulin.

The short answer to your questions about their reflection of medical reality:  minimal at best.

* In the interest of dramatic concentration, everything on TV is interesting and frequently rare (Lots of what are called "zebras."  (From the expression:  "If you hear hoofbeats in the distance, it's probably not a zebra.").   In a different formulation:  "Common things are common."  The reality is that the vast majority of things we see are humdrum and most things heal up fine without ever seeing a doctor.
* 50% of our time is spent charting.  You never see this on TV.

(to be continued)

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Sorry about the re-post.

(continued from the first post):

* Most resuscitations are a chaotic mess.  Even when you have a good team leader who assigns everyone their task at the beginning and good docs and nurses who know what they're doing, it's never as remotely seamless as TV depicts.  IV's and endo-tracheal tubes and medicines are never seconds away from being available and never already prepared.  It takes longer to crack a chest than 10 seconds.  Usually a few minutes in the nimblest hands.  No one is immediately intubated.  The list of things like this is endless.
* There are no average or mediocre doctors in TV emergency departments.  Everyone's either brilliant or hobbled by dementia or PTSD or drugs.  The reality is most are average and often frustratingly so.   Many are just dumb.  Like in any work setting.
* Tests take hours to come back in a busy ED.  On TV it's minutes because they only have an hour and waiting is boring.
* Most of the knowledge being tested and imparted in TV emergency departments is straight-up textbook fodder and, hence, frequently not relevant.  There is a saying:  "Nothing presents like the textbook."  And it's true by and large.  The real teaching done by good and seasoned docs is much more nuts-and-bolts and not to be found in books.

@Andrzej
The IRR- opening leads an English speaker's etymological eye to see an elided Latin IN- prefix plus a root starting with R. Just as IN + MOBILE becomes IMMOBILE, IN + RESPONSIBLE  becomes IRRESPONSIBLE, etc. The long I and single R at the start of IRATE would preclude such an etymology.

@Andrzej
The way the Germans use the word “irritieren” confounded me for quite a while. It isn’t used to signify anger, but rather some sort of confusion or puzzlement — perhaps being  “stirred” in some way.

@Ned S

I'm no biologist but as I understand it, a DNA double helix consists of two intertwined DNA strands.

@Ned S

Oh oh.  You unlocked my mansplaining box.

No, not quite.

First of all, RNA and DNA, both have a double helix structure.  Most of the components that go into making DNA and RNA are the same, but the sugar is different.  In RNA is is ribose, and in DNA it is deoxy-ribose (there should be a number there, to indicate where the "deoxy" part is, but I don't remember it).

So one of the RNA building blocks is different from the DNA building blocks (there are also so other differences in the bases used).

So a DNA molecule is the full double helix.  These strands are held together by a whole lot of fairly weak forces, and so it is possible to "unzip" the molecule into the two helices.  Various processes do this unzipping, and then use these two helices to construct as a template for creating two more helices out of raw materials.  

Afterwords, we have to identical molecules, which encodes enough information to make a cougar, or an emu, or a sequoia tree, or a potoroo, or a terrible, terrible mansplainer. 

Note:

Someone double check me here.  I'm going off of what I learned a long time ago, and I'm too lazy to go out and refresh.

So if I've misstated something, let me know.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Francis

You shouldn't let strangers touch your mansplaining box without your permission.

@Francis
1) RNA is not generally double-stranded, and it is not not helical.
2) DNA is composed of four base units, called nucleotides, abbreviated A,G,T and G.  RNA is composed of A, G, U and G.  The presence of U (uracil) instead of T (thymine) is, I think, the different building block you were thinking of.

You are correct that the nucleotides of RNA each contain a ribose sugar instead of a deoxyribose.

@Ned S

Interesting.  Perhaps this misunderstanding of RNA is why certain anti-vaxxers mistakenly think mRNA vaccines can change their DNA.  (For the record, it can't.)
@Chungclan trying to understand antivaxxers is a waste of time. It’s a religion. Either you believe or not. There is no rationale behind religion.

@Joe

Thanks, Joe, for the double check.

We didn't spend much time on RNA back in the 70s, when I last encountered this stuff.

I probably should have brushed up before I mouthed off, but oh well...

@Ned S Two things that might help. First of all, I can say without the slightest bit of doubt that half of a DNA strand is NOT an RNA strand. RNA uses the sugar ribose in it's structure, and DNA uses a sugar deoxy-ribose in it's structure. Two different sugars with different structure and properties. Another thing that might help. If you visualize the DNA double helix, wrapping around like a fancy barber pole (remember those), all that holds the two helices together are relatively weak hydrogen bonds. Further more, when they separate, they *I believe* still maintain their helical structure. The wiki page has an illustration: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNA_replication

@Chungclan

I was going to make that very point.  Maybe a way of explaining it would be that both molecules contain base pair sequences of four different bases, which can be used to encode information.  

I believe that's why some of it is "messenger" RNA, in that it can be constructed in such a way as to convey in formation.

DNA does something very similar, except that the information encoded is specifically used to recreate the individual which is based on the DNA, and it is used purely for reproductive information.

commentBody:
@Down_Home

Nonsense.

MMA is far less barbaric than the so-called "sweet science" (fights are stopped at the first sign of a combatant's inability to intelligently defend him- or herself, as opposed to the  use of standing eight counts (i.e. opportunities to sustain, absorb and marginally and temporarily recover from brain damage and return to combat) and much lovelier to watch, given its melange of disciplines and inclusion of submission fighting.

Demonstrably fewer concussions and as yet we've seen no MMA fighters with post-mortem evidence of CTE and no public behaviors consistent with the condition.

Facts matter.

@Down_Home
And also appeals to MAGA-types.
